bridge_lemma: Support-closure lemma: there exists an absolute K such that every unitary perfect n has ω(n) ≤ K, reducing unbounded existence to bounded support search. Mechanism: define the UnitaryDependencyGraph G(n) with vertices primeFactors(n) and edges p → q iff q | 1+p^{v_p(n)}; the identity ∏(1+p^{a_p}) = 2∏p^{a_p} forces G(n) support-closed, while BHV (Lucas pair (p,-1)) guarantees primitive divisors of 1+p^a are pairwise distinct and exceed 2a, forcing |V| to explode beyond Wall's fixed-k regime.
informal_proof_outline: Assume for contradiction that infinitely many unitary perfect numbers exist. By Aristotle's prior result (already in the Lean file) every such n is even; write n = 2^{a_2} · ∏_{i=1}^k p_i^{a_i} with odd primes p_1 < ... < p_k and let V := {2, p_1, ..., p_k} be its prime support. Step 1 (multiplicative identity): unitary perfection σ*(n) = 2n is equivalent to ∏_{p ∈ V} (1 + p^{v_p(n)}) = 2 · ∏_{p ∈ V} p^{v_p(n)}. Hence every prime divisor of every factor 1 + p^{v_p(n)} must already lie in V. Step 2 (UnitaryDependencyGraph): define G(n) as the directed multigraph on V with an edge p → q (multiplicity v_q(1+p^{v_p(n)})) for every q | (1+p^{v_p(n)}). By Step 1 this graph is support-closed: no edge leaves V. By the valuation identity v_q(2n) = v_q(∏(1+p^{a_p})), the in-degrees and out-degrees satisfy a global balance equation Σ_{p ∈ V} v_q(1+p^{a_p}) = v_q(2n) for every q ∈ V. Step 3 (primitive divisor obstruction): apply Bilu-Hanrot-Voutier (Math. Ann. 316, 2001 / arXiv math/9911035) to the Lucas sequence u_n = (x^n - y^n)/(x - y) with x = p, y = -1, which makes 1 + p^a a primitive divisor target. BHV guarantees that for every a > 30 the term 1 + p^a has a primitive prime divisor q∗ whose multiplicative order modulo q∗ equals exactly 2a — in particular q∗ ≡ 1 (mod 2a), so q∗ > 2a. Step 4 (support explosion): if k = |V| - 1 is large, the BHV witnesses q∗_i := primitive divisor of 1 + p_i^{a_i} are pairwise distinct (their associated orders 2a_i differ) and each q∗_i > 2 min(a_i). The support-closure constraint q∗_i ∈ V forces V to contain at least k primes each larger than the corresponding 2a_i. Combined with the geometric inequality ∏(1 + 1/p_i) > ∏_{i ≤ k}(1 + 1/(2a_i)) > 2 for k large enough relative to the a_i, we reach a contradiction with the identity ∏(1 + p_i^{-a_i}) = 2. Step 5 (residual finiteness via Wall): the BHV obstruction together with the support-closure constraint forces k ≤ K for an absolute computable constant K (extractable from the BHV exception table for n ≤ 30 plus a height bound from Step 4). For each fixed k ≤ K, Wall's 1972 theorem supplies finiteness of unitary perfect numbers with exactly k prime factors. The finite union of finite sets is finite, completing the proof.
candidate_lemmas: unitarySigma_eq_two_n_iff: n is unitary perfect iff ∏_{p ∈ primeFactors n}(1 + p^{n.factorization p}) = 2 * n (this is already proven in the Lean file as unitarySigma_eq_prod_prime_factors combined with isUnitaryPerfect_iff_unitarySigma_eq_two_mul).
support_closure: if n is unitary perfect and q is a prime with q ∣ (1 + p^{n.factorization p}) for some p ∈ primeFactors n, then q ∈ primeFactors n ∪ {2}.

bhv_primitive_divisor: for any prime p and exponent a > 30, the integer 1 + p^a admits a prime divisor q∗ whose order modulo q∗ is exactly 2a (Bilu-Hanrot-Voutier 2001 specialized to Lucas pair (p, -1)).
primitive_divisor_size: any such q∗ satisfies q∗ ≡ 1 (mod 2a), hence q∗ > 2a.

support_explosion: if n is unitary perfect with primeFactors n = {p_1 < ... < p_k} and v_{p_i}(n) > 30 for at least k₀ indices, then the BHV primitive divisors q∗_i for those indices are distinct and each exceeds 2·v_{p_i}(n), forcing k ≥ k₀.

abundancy_product_bound: ∏(1 + 1/p_i) > 2 fails for all sufficiently large k regardless of the choice of p_i (this is a real-analysis bound, since ∏(1 + 1/p_i) ≤ ∏(1 + 1/p_i^{a_i}) only when a_i = 1, and forcing larger exponents makes the LHS smaller).
k_bounded: there exists an absolute constant K (computable from the BHV exception table) such that any unitary perfect n satisfies ω(n) ≤ K.
wall_fixed_k_finite: for each fixed k ≥ 1, the set {n : IsUnitaryPerfect n ∧ ω(n) = k} is finite (Wall 1972).
erdos_1052_finite: combining k_bounded with wall_fixed_k_finite gives {n : IsUnitaryPerfect n}.Finite as a finite union of finite sets.
honest_disclaimer: 6 prior Aristotle attempts on E1052 all failed (slot306, slot307, slot308, slot353, slot354, slot355, slot567, slot623, slot692 — the most recent compiled but advanced nothing). The cross-domain machinery here is HEAVY: Bilu-Hanrot-Voutier's full primitive-divisor theorem is not in Mathlib (only the elementary Bang-Zsigmondy case for a^n - b^n exists in pieces), the order-mod-q∗ control for 1 + p^a requires linear forms in logarithms (Baker theory, also not formalized), and the abundancy_product_bound is a real-analysis lemma that requires careful inequality manipulation. The fusion lane CAN deliver: a structured strategy with explicit named techniques (BHV + Wall + support-closure graph), specific candidate lemmas, and the UnitaryDependencyGraph object that Gemini coined to encode the support-closure constraint as a graph-theoretic invariant. The fusion lane CANNOT: pre-verify that BHV transfers cleanly without an effective version of Baker's bound, or that Aristotle can synthesize a multi-step proof spanning analytic number theory, combinatorics, and Lucas sequences. fit_score = 0.32 reflects six prior failures plus the unimplemented Mathlib infrastructure. Plausibility of full closure: ~0.15; plausibility of a meaningful sub-claim (e.g. bounded-ω closure for ω ≤ K explicit) ~0.35.
